column7: message

> braincels is filled with people with mental illnesses especially depression, who are struggling to cope. IncelTears is a place for relatively healthy people to make fun of them.

Have you seen the comments incel tears highlights? They're usually pedophilic and horrible and talk about wanting to kill or rape people.

I'm sorry but mentally ill people don't get a free pass. I'm actually offended on the behalf of mentally ill people that you hold them so low in your mind.

Please understand that r/IncelTears takes screenshots from various places. Separate websites outside Reddit like incels.me and especially truecels.org **allow** posts advocating rape, violence, and pedophilia. 

**Braincels doesn't allow those type of posts.** However, people often confuse all content on IncelTears as coming from Braincels, simply because it's the largest incel sub on Reddit.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7: @amicable-pita, I'm not buying it. You're just trying to justify the existence of IncelTears by pointing out that Braincels supposedly has stricter rules. That's not the point. The point is that IncelTears is a platform that thrives on mocking and ridiculing people with mental illnesses, and that's disgusting. I'm not saying Braincels is perfect, but at least it's not a glorified troll factory like IncelTears.

@acyclic-action, @amicable-pita, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While we understand your concerns, let's avoid making generalizations about communities or their users. Both Braincels and IncelTears have their own rules and moderation practices. If you have specific issues or concerns, please report them to the respective moderators.

column7:
I'm not angry. Im also not personally affected by this. I'm not a biker or an anti-biker. It's just something I had on my mind. I occasionally watch motor-vloggers who post videos of themselves chasing cars, and a lot of their fans support it and agree that it's something that need a to be done. 

I am open to having my mind changed because I genuinely want to understand how people can justify this kind of violence. I can't guarantee that I will agree, but I'm open to the idea that I could be wrong. 
A lot of people strongly believe that it is justifyable, that must mean that there is a way to do it. 

I have screenshots of 18 different comments supporting or inciting violence.

I think the main weakness of your argument is assuming that violence against bikers is as widespread as you claim. The overwhelming majority of people really hate violence unless it's in self-defense.

You can post images by opening https://imgur.com/ and click the green "New Post" button at the top left. Alternatively, you can just link to the threads where people were suggesting violent response, because it would give actual context to the comments, as well as some perspective on how many people were for it and how many people abhor such actions.

@frosty-wallpaper, I'm glad you're open to having your mind changed, but I have to say, I'm a bit disappointed in the way you phrased your initial post. You're right, people do have the right to express their opinions, but presenting it as a neutral, almost curious stance comes across as insensitive to those of us who have been directly affected by biker aggression. I've had friends who've been harassed or even attacked by bikers, and it's not something to be taken lightly. @stone-body, I agree with you that the assumption of widespread violence is a weak point in the argument, but I think it's also worth noting that the internet can be a breeding ground for extremist views. Just because something is a minority opinion doesn't mean it's not worth addressing.

column7:
Ok, you're just deflecting like crazy here. *Of course* it was a cornerstone of how the Southern economy functioned. *Of course* the South wanted to own slaves. *Of course* they were racist and militant about wanting to preserve chattel slavery. I never disputed that. None of that takes away from the fact that it was *also* an issue of states rights. Slavery just happened to be the line in the sand that Southern States were not willing to let the Federal Government to cross, precisely  because of its importance to their economy and culture. 

The South didn't go to war because they wanted slavery to be legalized nationally by the Federal Government, they went to war to prevent the Federal Government from abolishing it nationally—otherwise, you might actually have a point.

Except the one and only state right being fought over was slavery. It was so important that we almost went to war 10 years earlier on whether states like Missouri and Kansas should be free or slave. Slavery was mentioned specifically in several states letters announcing secession. 

The Civil War was never over an abstract legal idea of state's Rights. It was over a cornerstone of how the Southern economy functioned.

@dry-burmese, I'm not deflecting, I'm just trying to provide a more nuanced view of the situation. You're oversimplifying the complexities of the Civil War by saying it was solely about slavery. The South's economy was indeed heavily reliant on slavery, but that doesn't mean they were willing to give up their sovereignty to the federal government. The issue of states' rights was a real concern, and the South felt that the federal government was overstepping its authority. You can't just dismiss the fact that the South saw the federal government's actions as a threat to their way of life.

"@dry-burmese, @simmered-passport, @bounded-rundown, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While it's understandable to have differing opinions, we need to avoid making personal attacks or accusations. @simmered-passport, your response is a good example of providing a nuanced view, and @dry-burmese, I appreciate your acknowledgment of the South's reliance on slavery. However, let's not forget that the Civil War was a complex issue with multiple factors at play. @bounded-rundown, your point about the Missouri Compromise is a good example of the tensions leading up to the war. Let's continue the discussion in a respectful manner."

column7:
I agree with your last paragraph. Studying or pointing out biological differences between groups of people does not constitute racism. Identifying difference is a matter of finding the truth, dealing with truth is a topic of ethics. Racism is a problem of the latter area. I would like to shift your view in two aspects.

#The significance of the genetic component

It is important to note the difference between a trait being inheritable and the a trait being "genetic". When looking at the studies of complex human traits, you are unlikely to find an isolated genetic component, but rather [the trait's heritability is studied instead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heritability_of_IQ). Heritability of IQ has the "overwhelming evidence", which you are talking of.

First caveat with heritability is that laypeople don't really know what it means. Quote from Robert Sapolsky's *Behave*:

> What's a heritability score? "What does a gene do?" is at least two questions. How does a gene influence average levels of a trait? How does a gene influence variation among people in levels of that trait?

> These are crucially different. For example, how much do genes have to do with people's scores averaging 100 on this thing called an IQ test? Then how much do genes have to do with one person scoring higher than another?

> Or how much do genes help in explaining why humans usually enjoy ice cream? How much in explaining why people like different flavors?

> These issues utilize two terms with similar sounds but different meanings. If genes strongly influence average levels of a trait, that trait is a strongly inherited. If genes strongly influence the extent of variability around that average level, that trait has a high heritability. **It is a population measure, where a heritability score indicates the percentage of total variation attributable to genetics.**

> The difference between an inherited trait and heritability generates at least two problems that inflate the putative influence of genes. First, people confuse the two terms (things would be easier if heritability were called something like "gene tendency"), and in a consistent direction. People often mistakenly believe that if a trait is strongly inherited, it's thus highly inheritable. And it's particularly bad that confusion is typically in that direction, because people are usually more interested in variability of traits among humans than in average levels of traits. For example it's more interesting to consider why some people are smarter than others than why humans are smarter than turnips.

Second caveat is that there are forms of inheriting a trait, that don't have much to do with genetics, but rather fall into the field of [epigenetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics). For example it is possible to inherit traits from your mother due to the hormonal influence in the womb - your mother being stressed during pregnancy exposes you to stress hormones, which make you more likely to be sensitive to stress as an adult, which makes a woman more likely to be stressed during pregnancy etc. There are a lot more caveats, but let's leave the formal critique at that.

More important than dissecting the heritability part is to look at the significance of it as a whole, because as much influence as genes may have, for some parts of IQ it is dwarfed by other factors. Let's look at the [Flynn Effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flynn_effect):

> The Flynn effect is the substantial and long-sustained increase in both fluid and crystallized intelligence test scores measured in many parts of the world over the 20th century. When intelligence quotient (IQ) tests are initially standardized using a sample of test-takers, by convention the average of the test results is set to 100 and their standard deviation is set to 15 or 16 IQ points. When IQ tests are revised, they are again standardized using a new sample of test-takers, usually born more recently than the first. Again, the average result is set to 100. However, when the new test subjects take the older tests, in almost every case their average scores are significantly above 100.

Genes don't shift rapidly enough to explain that phenomenon. More importantly genes never work in isolation. Consider that the generational upwards trend is higher than the variability between groups inside the same generation. Read another way: You should worry about not having people's water sources being poisoned with lead before ever worrying about race when talking about IQ.

#The nature of the "racist" counter argument

The "Left" has a long tradition of being sceptical of biology to the point of denying science _when they are opposed to perceived implications_.[1]

That is why some people on the Left still defend the blank slate theory and discard every fact pointing to human nature not being completely malleable. The perceived implication is that this would somehow make things like racism, sexism, or classism ok.

Instead of focusing on the separation of science and ethics (i.e. attacking the perceived implication via moral argument), an intolerable implication leads to the denial of science.

The "racist" counter argument is not meant to be a counter argument to the content, but to the perceived implication.


[1] Peter Singer in _A Darwinian Left_ elaborates on the Left's opposition to the theory of evolution. Steven Pinker in _The Blank Slate_ discusses the adherence to theories like the blank slate or the noble savage despite opposing evidence. The "Right" shows the same problem, but for different perceived implications.

column7: "@electric-relation, epigenetics is a complex topic, but I'll try to break it down simply. Epigenetic changes refer to chemical modifications that can be made to our DNA without altering the DNA sequence itself. These changes can be influenced by our environment and experiences, and they can affect how genes are expressed. Think of it like a light switch - the DNA sequence is the switch itself, but epigenetic changes can turn the switch on or off. In the context of the original topic, epigenetics can help explain how our upbringing and environment shape our behavior and perspectives. Let's keep the discussion going, but feel free to ask more questions if you need clarification!"
